name: Michael Lee
tagline: M.S. Analytics / Georgia Tech  /  B.S. Information Science / UMD
bio: Product manager and builder with experience in customer discovery, product strategy, and end-to-end product delivery. I use hypothesis-driven iteration and real user feedback to validate product decisions before scaling engineering investment. Background in USAF with experience spanning enterprise ERP delivery, data consulting, and SaaS product development.

projects.name: ReviewStar
projects.problem: Job seekers practice behavioral interviews without structured feedback and generic advice doesn't help them improve at the STAR level.
projects.evidence: Defined product vision and managed the full lifecycle from discovery through 7 iterative releases. Built a full-stack SaaS with a FastAPI backend, Next.js frontend, Supabase auth and PostgreSQL, Google OAuth, mic or text input, and LLaMA 3.3 70B via Groq for per-section STAR scoring and answer rewrites. Implemented tiered usage limits, Stripe live subscriptions, and a Pro session history dashboard.
projects.result: Live at reviewstar.cv. Shipped 7 product updates, drove 40% active user engagement with the streak system, and pivoted from an ad-supported model to subscription-first after analyzing user behavior and engagement data.
highlights: Scores each STAR section individually with per-section feedback and a full answer rewrite in a single Groq API call
Tiered rate-limiting: IP-based for anonymous users and Supabase-persisted monthly counts for logged-in tiers
Streak bonus system with daily login credits and milestone tracking drives 40% active user engagement; feature roadmap built across 7 releases from direct user feedback
Practice Session mode queues multiple questions scored independently with a Pro session history dashboard and trend charts
Next.js static export served directly by FastAPI/uvicorn on AWS EC2 Ubuntu with systemd service management
Live Stripe recurring billing at $9/mo with webhook-driven plan upgrades reflected in Supabase profiles

projects.name: AI Labor Market Risk Pipeline
projects.problem: No model existed to correlate AI-exposure risk with financial vulnerability across occupations, making it difficult to assess credit risk in AI-disrupted labor markets.
projects.evidence: Built an end-to-end pipeline ingesting from BLS OEWS, Anthropic Economic Index, O*NET, and Census ACS PUMS into Snowflake via Terraform-provisioned AWS S3. Implemented comprehensive dbt tests across staging, intermediate, and mart layers with a composite score weighting AI exposure against financial vulnerability.
projects.result: MART_CREDIT_RISK_BY_OCCUPATION ranks every occupation by composite risk score, with Medical Transcriptionists surfacing as the highest-risk occupation.
highlights: Multi-source ETL ingesting from BLS OEWS, Anthropic Economic Index, O*NET, and Census ACS PUMS into Snowflake
Composite score weighting AI automation exposure against financial vulnerability indicators including unemployment, poverty, and income instability
Financial vulnerability aggregated from Census ACS microdata across multiple economic stress indicators per occupation
Comprehensive dbt test suite across staging, intermediate, and mart layers covering nulls, uniqueness, range checks, and accepted values
Resolved multiple real-world integration bugs including Anthropic dataset restructures, Census URL migrations, and Snowflake COPY INTO idempotency
Terraform IaC: S3 bucket, IAM role with Snowflake trust policy via external ID, SNS alerting

projects.name: DoD Small Business Contract Pipeline
projects.problem: Federal contract data from USASpending.gov is large, paginated, and raw with no easy way to track DoD small business trends or benchmark vendors.
projects.evidence: Built an end-to-end ETL with paginated REST API ingestion, pandas transforms with NAICS classification and deduplication, PostgreSQL load via SQLAlchemy 2.0, and an interactive Streamlit dashboard with KPI cards, choropleth map, and sidebar filters. Extended to Azure Blob Storage and Azure SQL.
projects.result: Fully functional analytics platform over federal contract data usable for vendor research and competitive landscape analysis.
highlights: Paginated ingestion from USASpending.gov with multiple validation checks and a configurable pass-rate threshold
Pipeline audit table tracks records in, records out, pass rate, and status per run
Dashboard includes KPI cards, monthly volume chart, NAICS sector spending, top recipients, award tier breakdown, and a choropleth map
Sidebar filters for sub-agency, NAICS sector, and award tier applied dynamically across all visualizations
Dry-run mode extracts and transforms without writing to the database
Architecture maps to Azure DP-900 scope: Blob Storage, Data Factory, and Azure SQL

projects.name: Game Sales Data Analysis
projects.problem: Market intelligence for video game publishers, built from raw sales records. The starting point was a flat CSV with 1,900 game titles and no way to ask it a real business question. You could see what sold, but not where to compete, which genres were worth entering, or whether localizing for Japan would pay off.
projects.evidence: I started by figuring out what questions a publisher strategy team would actually need answered. From there I designed a normalized MySQL database across six relational tables and cleaned the raw records for missing values, duplicates, and inconsistent formatting. That structure made it possible to run queries across regions, genres, and publishers in ways the flat file never could.
projects.result: The bigger push was turning the analysis into actual decisions. I built a genre entry scorecard that scores every genre across five factors: per-title sales efficiency, market saturation, Western market accessibility, quality-to-sales correlation, and recent growth momentum. Each genre comes out with a GO, CONSIDER, or AVOID verdict. I also built a Japan localization model that looks at each genre revenue share relative to the market average and gives a straight YES or NO based on whether the numbers justify the cost. The final report wraps everything into four written recommendations that take a real position on where to compete, how to budget, and what the post-2012 sales decline actually means for a publisher.
highlights: Normalized relational schema replacing a flat CSV with six queryable tables
Cleaned 1,900 records with null handling, deduplication, and type normalization
Genre entry scorecard scores all genres and outputs explicit GO / CONSIDER / AVOID verdicts
Japan localization model gives per-genre YES or NO decisions based on revenue index vs. cost threshold
Interactive HTML report with 9 sections moves from raw data to prescriptive strategy

projects.name: iConsultancy: Nonprofit Data Analysis
projects.problem: A nonprofit had inconsistent donor and transaction data with no framework for data-driven operational decisions and no visibility into workflow inefficiencies.
projects.evidence: Mapped donor management and transaction workflows end-to-end, created user journey maps from stakeholder interviews to identify pain points, built donor segmentation analysis across giving frequency, recency, and amount, and synthesized quantitative trends with qualitative findings into an executive briefing presented to client leadership.
projects.result: Delivered an executive briefing formally reviewed and accepted by client leadership, translating findings into prioritized workflow redesign recommendations for strategic planning and budget decisions.
highlights: Mapped donor management and transaction workflows end-to-end to identify operational bottlenecks
User journey maps built from direct stakeholder interviews to surface pain points and drive workflow redesign decisions
Donor segmentation analysis by giving frequency, recency, and amount
Identified data governance and PII handling gaps in existing workflows

projects.name: USG iSchool Chatbot
projects.problem: An in-progress university chatbot lacked alignment between stakeholder expectations, student needs, and actual response quality.
projects.evidence: Joined mid-project and partnered with advisors, IT, and departmental stakeholders to define success criteria; analyzed the RAG-based LLM architecture to improve context structuring; and moderated quality using like/dislike feedback signals to refine response guidelines.
projects.result: Improved satisfaction from a 65/35 to 95/5 like-to-dislike ratio through iterative, feedback-driven response improvements over the engagement period.
highlights: Brought in partway through the project to help improve a chatbot already being used by students
Talked to advisors, IT staff, and department contacts to understand what the chatbot needed to do better
Found that the chatbot was pulling in the wrong context to answer questions, and flagged fixes to improve accuracy
Student satisfaction went from 65% positive to 95% positive through repeated rounds of testing and adjustments

projects.name: Fur-ever Care
projects.problem: Pet owners reported significant cleaning fatigue from manual litter maintenance, but existing automated products were either unreliable or too expensive, leaving demand unmet for an affordable, low-maintenance option.
projects.evidence: Led user research and prototype testing for a five-person team designing an affordable, app-connected automated cat litter disposal box. Conducted interviews with 7 participants across two segments (college students and adult pet owners), then synthesized findings into an affinity diagram surfacing cleaning fatigue, smart tech skepticism, and care-versus-automation preferences as the three core design constraints. Developed three user personas grounded in research and co-designed a low-fidelity prototype featuring a weight-sensing disposal trigger, in-app manual override, monitoring camera, and passive cooling system. Ran structured usability walkthroughs using a five-scenario scoring instrument with think-aloud protocol.
projects.result: Testing confirmed the core disposal mechanism was intuitive across participants, while camera and tracking features surfaced as the primary usability gap requiring redesign. Findings validated the concept and produced a prioritized feature recommendation, identifying odor control as the highest-impact next addition based on testing gaps and qualitative user feedback.
highlights: Ran think-aloud usability sessions with 7 participants across two segments (college students and adult pet owners), scoring 5 product scenarios on an ease/difficulty/not-done scale
Synthesized interview data into an affinity diagram surfacing cleaning fatigue, smart tech skepticism, and care-vs-automation preferences as three interconnected design constraints
Defined three user personas (time-constrained executive, college student, overworked nurse) to align the team on target use cases before any prototyping began
Camera and tracking scored majority with-difficulty ratings across testers; core cleaning mechanism scored majority with-ease, validating the disposal concept while flagging camera UX for redesign
Recommended odor control as the highest-priority next feature based on testing gaps and qualitative feedback about smell as an unaddressed pain point not covered by the prototype
